intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users of health dashboards prefer vertical navigation paired with card-based content blocks over tabbed interfaces
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation pattern + Content grouping (sidebar vs tabs, cards vs lists)
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: Explore more sidebar-card combinations; test depth of sidebar sections; probe card density and spacing
facade.rejectImplies: Test tab-based navigation; explore list layouts; probe horizontal organization patterns

facade.hypothesis: A friendly, conversational tone with progress celebration (vs clinical data logging) will feel more motivating for sustained habit tracking
facade.label: Cheerful check-ins
facade.acceptImplies: User wants warmth and encouragement woven into the tracking experience—celebrate wins, use uplifting language, maybe light emoji or illustrations
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers clean, neutral, matter-of-fact data entry—no emotional framing, just the facts and numbers

facade.hypothesis: The user prefers rounded, flowing visual language (warm aesthetic) over sharp, geometric precision
facade.label: Soft curves
facade.acceptImplies: favor organic, gentle shapes; likely values approachability and ease in the interface
facade.rejectImplies: favor clean lines and structure; prioritize clarity and precision over visual softness

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer animated data transitions that show change over time rather than instant number swaps
facade.label: Smooth sliding graphs
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should use easing curves and interpolated transitions when metrics update—build motion into the core feedback loop
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard should prioritize instant clarity over motion—show latest numbers immediately without animation delays

evidence.hypothesis: Users of health dashboards prefer vertical navigation paired with card-based content blocks over tabbed interfaces
evidence.implication: Explore more sidebar-card combinations; test depth of sidebar sections; probe card density and spacing

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments may prefer gentle, approachable UI elements over clinical or rigid layouts, reducing cognitive friction around personal health data.
facade.label: Soft rounded cards
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into organic, cushioned corners and soft edges throughout the dashboard; consider curved containers for charts and metric displays.
facade.rejectImplies: Explore cleaner, more angular or minimalist card structures; possibly flat or sharp-cornered layouts that feel more data-driven and less decorative.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

facade.hypothesis: For personal health experiments, showing key metrics as large, scannable numbers (weight, sleep hours, workout count) in card format will be more motivating and faster to parse than tables or detailed charts.
facade.label: Big number cards
facade.acceptImplies: User wants prominent, at-a-glance summary stats. Likely prefers cards or tiles over tables. May want colorful badges or progress rings alongside numbers.
facade.rejectImplies: User finds isolated metrics context-poor. Likely wants to see relationships between variables (correlation, trends, causation). May prefer charts, comparisons, or narrative context over bare stats.

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer subtle, ambient background layers (soft gradients or color washes) over stark white or neutral backgrounds in health dashboards, creating a sense of comfort and reduced visual fatigue during frequent check-ins
facade.axisTargeted: Visual warmth and ambient space—whether the interface feels clinical/sterile vs. calming/enveloping
facade.label: Warm background glow
facade.acceptImplies: Explore warm color palettes, layered depth, soft lighting effects; test if ambient backgrounds reduce eye strain during repeated daily interactions
facade.rejectImplies: User leans toward clarity over coziness; test crisp, high-contrast backgrounds and minimal ornamentation instead
